text: The robotic surgical system is docked, and an initial inspection of the abdominal cavity is conducted, revealing the anatomical structures and any visible abnormalities.
The surgeon uses two grasping forceps to carefully reduce the omentum and transverse colon from the hernia sac, applying gentle downward traction to reposition the hernia contents.
After the complete reduction of the hernia, the surgeon inspects the omentum and transverse colon, confirming there is no visible ischemia or damage to these structures.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum in a linear fashion, followed by sharp and blunt dissection to create a preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon performs dissection from lateral to medial to address the herniated defect, followed by the reduction of the hernia sac and preperitoneal fat using surgical instruments.
The surgeon extends the preperitoneal plane by continuing the dissection laterally for several centimeters, creating sufficient working space.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable suture to close the fascial defect, employing a running stitch technique.
The surgeon places a mesh centrally over the defect area, securing it with a central tagging suture. Interrupted vicryl sutures are then used circumferentially to anchor the mesh to the fascia.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal defect with a running absorbable suture after securing the mesh over the defect area.
